author: @maintoc
text: Very interesting, and bravo.  :)   Not too many years ago, an Isreali company was working on low-cost (and almost completely recyclable, when worn out) bicycles made out of cardboard that had been treated with chemicals to make the cardboard stronger.  The bike had no chain and gears but, rather, a sort of belt drive.  Sadly, for whatever reason/s, the company never got their factory going.

author: @YeshuaGod22
text: Brilliant! I've really enjoyed the way this project has challenged me to think about the forces involved in bike riding and frame geometry. I'm currently sitting on the opinion that the most severe, sudden forces on a gently ridden bike are when dropping off a kerb, when a grown man will probably push over 1000N momentarily straight down through the pedals/footpegs/crankshaft. Does that seem about right to you?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest it's probably on braking - the steering column head is quite fragile and a 80kg cyclist can reach 30-35 kph quite easily

replies.author: @YeshuaGod22
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I wonder how much brake bias affects the amount of force there? I'd imagine using a front brake alone would compel the front wheel towards the seat tube much more vigorously than using a rear brake alone.

author: @guillermodelnoche

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thanks for sharing Rob et al. Excellent idea using ‘paper’. I’ve also seen articles where wood-pulp is shredded to ‘nano- fibres’ and reformed to rival steel strengths. Have you considered making/using this material in your DIY ideas?
Hmm. Could you make the frame a rolled paper EESD?
Also, the rake and projection of the forks is so your toes don’t collide with the back of the front wheel when steering. Since you have gone electric (sans pedals), you can adjust the frame shorter and lower (lighter/compact), still maintain 500w+ for ‘sportiness’, and make it a commuter carry-on/folder. No need to lock it up outside where it might get nicked.
Stick a VAWT on your hat and passing vehicles will recharge your EESD. And it's a visual safety aid.   %-)
You’ll be well fed-up with people telling you the ‘goose neck’ is backwards. So I wont. ;-)

author: @morski_kapetan
text: First - I never new paper had grains? Second - I am amazed at how strong the paper tubes are. Thirdly, this is a revolutionary thing - if people knew this, paper would never end up at the trash bins. We should have paper furniture, paper boats, paper everything? If only paper were made out of hemp, not the trees...we would have a perfect society, beautiful forests.
